analogy: Think of "US Hegemony" through the chapter's own opening images: O verview Let us shift our gaze from the larger global developments in the post- Cold War era to developments in our own region, South Asia. When India and Pakistan joined the club of nuclear powers, this region suddenly became the focus of global attention.... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 31 Contemporary South Asia, TIMELINE OF SOUTH ASIA, SINCE 1947, 1971 March: Proclamation of Independence by leaders of. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
US Hegemony is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that o verview Let us shift our gaze from the larger global developments in the post- Cold War era to developments in our own region, South Asia.

The text explains that when India and Pakistan joined the club of nuclear powers, this region suddenly became the focus of global attention.

The text explains that the focus was, of course, on the various kinds of conflict in this region: there are pending border and water sharing disputes between the states of the region.

The text explains that besides, there are conflicts arising out of insurgency, ethnic strife and resource sharing.

The text explains that at the same time, many people in South Asia recognise the fact that this region can develop and prosper if the states of the region cooperate with each other.

The text explains that in this chapter, we try to understand the nature of conflict and cooperation among different countries of the region.

The text explains that since much of this is rooted in or conditioned by the domestic politics of these countries, we first introduce the region and the domestic politics of some of the big countries in the region.

The text explains that chapter 3 Contemporary South Asia Source: Subhas Rai’s adaptation of ‘Liberty Leading the People’, painted by Eugene Delacroix in 1830.

The text explains that courtesy of Himal Southasian, (January 2007) The Southasia Trust, Nepal

The text explains that contemporary World Politics 30 What is south asia?

The text explains that we are all familiar with the gripping tension during an India-Pakistan cricket match.

The text explains that we have also seen the goodwill and hospitality shown to visiting Indian and Pakistani fans by their hosts when they come to watch a cricket match.

The text explains that this is symbolic of the larger pattern of South Asian affairs.

The text explains that ours is a region where rivalry and goodwill, hope and despair, mutual suspicion and trust coexist.

The text explains that let us begin by asking an elementary question: what is South Asia?

The text explains that the expression ‘South Asia’ usually includes the following countries: Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, the Maldives, Nepal, Pakistan and Sri Lanka.

The text explains that in this chapter, we shall use South Asia to mean the seven countries mentioned above.

The text explains that the various countries in South Asia do not have the same kind of political systems.

The text explains that despite many problems and limitations, Sri Lanka and India have successfully operated a democratic system since their independence from the British.

The text explains that pakistan began the post- Cold War period with successive democratic governments under B e n a z i r B h u t t o a n d N a w a z S h a r i f r e s p e c t i v e l y .

The text explains that it has been run by a civilian government again since 2008.

The text explains that till 2006, Nepal was a constitutional monarchy with the danger of the king taking over executive powers.

The text explains that identify some features common to all the South Asian countries but different from countries in West Asia or Southeast Asia.

The text explains that bhutan became a constitutional monarchy in 2008.

The text explains that the Maldives, the other island nation, was a Sultanate till 1968 when it was transformed into a republic with a presidential form of government.

The text explains that in June 2005, the parliament of the Maldives voted unanimously to introduce a multi-party system.

The text explains that they prefer democracy over any other form of government and think that democracy is suitable for their country.

The text explains that source: SDSA Team, State of Democracy in South Asia, New Delhi: Oxford University Press, 2007 Countries SDG 3 Life expectancy at birth (years) 2017 SDG 4.6 Adult literacy rate (% ages 15 and older) 2006-2016 SDG 4.1 Gross enrolment ratio (Secondary) 2012-2017 SDG 8.1 GDP per capita (2011 PPP $) 2017 SDG 3.2 Infant mortality rate (per 1,000 live births) 2016 SDG 3.3 TB cases (per 100,000 people) 2016 SDG 1.1 Population living below income poverty line (%) PPP $1.90 a day 2006-2016 HDI Rank World 72.2 82.1 79 15,439 29.9 140.0 – – Developing 70.7 81.1 75 10,199 32.7 164.5 – – countries South Asia 69.3 68.7 71 6,485 37.8 206.3 – – Bangladesh 72.8 72.8 69 3,524 28.2 221.0 14.8 136 India 68.8 69.3 75 6,427 34.6 211.0 21.2 130 Nepal 70.6 59.6 71 2,433 28.4 154.0 15.0 149 Pakistan 66.6 57.0 46 5,035 64.2 268.0 6.1 150 Sri Lanka 75.5 91.2 98 11,669 8.0 65.0 – 76 Source: United Nations Development Programme, Human Development Report, 2018

The text explains that let us look at the experience of democracy in each of the four big countries of the region other than India. the Military and deMocracy in Pakistan After Pakistan framed its first constitution, General Ayub Khan took over the administration of the country and soon got himself elected.

The text explains that after this, an elected government under the leadership of Zulfikar Ali Bhutto came to power in Pakistan from 1971 to 1977.

The text explains that the Bhutto government was removed by General Zia- ul-Haq in 1977.

The text explains that general Zia faced a pro-democracy movement from 1982 onwards and an elected democratic government was established once again in 1988 under the leadership of Benazir Bhutto.

The text explains that the social dominance of the military, clergy, and landowning aristocracy has led to the frequent overthrow of elected governments and the establishment of military government.

The text explains that pakistan has a courageous and relatively free press and a strong human rights movement.

The text explains that the United States and other Western countries have encouraged the military’s authoritarian rule in the past, for their own reasons.

The text explains that but the government dominated by the West Pakistani leadership refused to convene the assembly.

The text explains that the government of India supported the demand of the people of East Pakistan for their independence and helped them financially and militarily.

The text explains that 35 Contemporary South Asia B a n g l a d e s h d r a f t e d i t s constitution declaring faith in secularism, democracy and socialism.

The text explains that however, in 1975 Sheikh Mujib got the constitution amended to shift fr om the parliamentary to presidential form of government.

The text explains that monarchy and deMocracy in nePal Nepal was a Hindu kingdom in the past and then a constitutional monarchy in the modern period for many years.

The text explains that throughout this period, political parties and the common people of Nepal have wanted a more open and responsive system of government.

The text explains that but the king, with the help of the army, retained full control over the government and restricted the expansion of democracy in Nepal.

The text explains that the king accepted the demand for a new democratic constitution in 1990, in the wake of a strong pro- democracy movement.

The text explains that however, democratic governments had a short and troubled career.

The text explains that in 2002, the king abolished the parliament and dismissed the government, thus ending even the limited democracy that existed in Nepal.

The text explains that nepal has undergone a unique moment in its history because it formed a constituent assembly to Let’s know more about Bangladesh’s Grameen Bank.

The text explains that contemporary World Politics 36 draft the constitution for Nepal.

The text explains that they wanted the constitution to include the radical programmes of social and economic restructuring.

The text explains that what are some of the commonalities and differences between Bangladesh and Pakistan in their democratic experiences?

The text explains that name the principal players in the ethnic conflict in Sri Lanka.

The text explains that how do you assess the prospects of the resolution of this conflict?

The text explains that mention some of the recent agreements between India and Pakistan.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Contemporary World Politics, Chapter 3: US Hegemony

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
